items.q: How does a catalyst speed up a reaction?
items.correct: It provides an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy.
items.misconception: A catalyst lowers the activation energy of the original reaction pathway.
items.random_wrong: A catalyst gives extra kinetic energy to the reactant particles.
items.why: Textbook diagrams often show 'Ea lowered' without making clear it's a new pathway, leading students to think catalysts modify the original pathway.

items.q: Does a catalyst affect the position of equilibrium?
items.correct: No — it speeds up both the forward and reverse reactions equally, so equilibrium is reached faster but at the same position.
items.misconception: A catalyst shifts the equilibrium towards the products.
items.random_wrong: A catalyst increases the yield by being consumed in the forward reaction only.
items.why: Students associate 'catalyst' with 'helps the reaction' and assume that means 'pushes towards products' rather than 'speeds up both directions equally'.

items.q: At dynamic equilibrium, what is happening to the reactions?
items.correct: Forward and reverse reactions continue at equal rates; concentrations remain constant.
items.misconception: Both reactions stop because the system has reached balance.
items.random_wrong: The forward reaction stops once the reverse reaction begins.
items.why: The word 'equilibrium' connotes stillness, so students think reactions stop — they miss that equilibrium is dynamic at the particle level.

items.q: Which does NOT increase reaction rate?
items.why: Correct answer: Lower concentration. “Higher temperature” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's lowering the activation energy. “raising the activation energy” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: If 20 cm³ of gas is produced in 5 s, what's the average rate?
items.why: The real figure is 4 cm^3/s. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
